U.S. stock index futures were little changed Wednesday night after a mostly lower Wall Street session, as investors weighed escalating tensions between the U.S. and Iran that threatened to derail a temporary peace agreement. S&P 500 futures were flat, Nasdaq 100 futures rose 0.2%, and Dow futures were largely unchanged. 
Earlier, the Dow fell 1.1%, the S&P 500 slipped 0.3%, while the Nasdaq gained 0.2%, supported by strength in semiconductor stocks. Investor sentiment weakened after President Donald Trump said the ceasefire with Iran was effectively over, followed by reports of new U.S. strikes on Iranian targets and warnings of retaliation from Tehran. 
The renewed conflict pushed oil prices sharply higher, raising concerns about disruptions to shipping through the Strait of Hormuz and adding to inflation worries. Meanwhile, technology stocks outperformed, with Broadcom rallying after announcing a major chip supply agreement with Apple, while Nvidia also advanced

Airbnb is facing a lawsuit from the City of Los Angeles over alleged price gouging during the January 2025 Southern California wildfires, after a California judge ruled the case can proceed. The city alleges Airbnb violated California law and the Los Angeles municipal code by advertising and collecting rental prices that exceeded legal limits during the state of emergency, even though hosts set the listing prices. 
The lawsuit, filed in July, claims rental prices at more than 2,600 properties increased by more than the 10% cap permitted under California's anti-price-gouging law, which was triggered by Governor Gavin Newsom's emergency declaration and later extended multiple times. City officials also challenged Airbnb's Smart Pricing tool, arguing the company played an active role in determining and displaying rental rates during the crisis.

Amazon launched the Claude App Gateway for AWS, a self-managed control system that gives organizations centralized management of access, spending and policy enforcement for Claude Code and Claude Desktop applications. The gateway eliminates the need for individual cloud credentials and manual configuration, allowing enterprises to deploy it through Amazon Bedrock to keep data within AWS security boundaries or through Claude Platform on AWS for a native Claude experience. 
Built as a stateless container that runs on Amazon ECS, EKS or EC2 with a PostgreSQL backend, the gateway integrates with OpenID Connect identity providers and issues short-lived authentication tokens through single sign-on. It also manages identity administration, inference request routing, telemetry, spending controls and policy enforcement. Organizations can set daily, weekly and monthly spending limits by user, group or organization, with requests automatically blocked when thresholds are exceeded.

Blue Origin is reportedly raising about $10 billion in its first external funding round, valuing Jeff Bezos' space company at $130 billion, according to CNBC. Bezos is expected to contribute $2 billion, while hedge fund Coatue Management will invest around $4 billion, with the remaining $4 billion drawing strong interest from other large institutional investors. 
The fundraising follows SpaceX's record IPO last month, which raised nearly $86 billion and valued Elon Musk's company at roughly $2 trillion, highlighting continued investor appetite for the commercial space sector. 
Founded by Bezos in 2000, Blue Origin has historically been funded through sales of his Amazon shares and competes with SpaceX in heavy-lift launch vehicles, lunar landers, and satellite internet. The capital raise comes despite a setback in May, when a New Glenn rocket exploded during a static fire test in Florida.

OpenAI said it no longer recommends SWE-Bench Pro as a reliable benchmark for evaluating AI coding performance after an internal audit found that about 30% of its tasks contain design flaws that skew results. The company withdrew its previous recommendation, saying the benchmark has become increasingly unreliable as leading AI models rapidly improve. 
The audit examined 731 public tasks using AI research agents and independent reviews by five experienced software engineers. OpenAI found defects in 27%–34% of the dataset, including overly strict tests, vague or incomplete task descriptions, insufficient test coverage, and misleading prompts that can reject correct solutions or allow incomplete fixes to pass. The company also noted that many tasks are automatically generated from real software repositories, where issue descriptions, code changes, and unit tests are often misaligned.

U.S. stock index futures were little changed Wednesday night after a mostly lower Wall Street session, as investors weighed escalating tensions between the U.S. and Iran that threatened to derail a temporary peace agreement. S&P 500 futures were flat, Nasdaq 100 futures rose 0.2%, and Dow futures were largely unchanged. 
Earlier, the Dow fell 1.1%, the S&P 500 slipped 0.3%, while the Nasdaq gained 0.2%, supported by strength in semiconductor stocks. Investor sentiment weakened after President Donald Trump said the ceasefire with Iran was effectively over, followed by reports of new U.S. strikes on Iranian targets and warnings of retaliation from Tehran. 
The renewed conflict pushed oil prices sharply higher, raising concerns about disruptions to shipping through the Strait of Hormuz and adding to inflation worries. Meanwhile, technology stocks outperformed, with Broadcom rallying after announcing a major chip supply agreement with Apple, while Nvidia also advanced 
 $AVGO $AAPL $NVDA  $IWM

NVIDIA pushed back against reports claiming its Kyber NVL144 AI rack system—built around 144 next-generation Rubin Ultra GPUs—had been delayed until 2028, stating that development remains on schedule. The denial eased investor concerns after the earlier report triggered a broad semiconductor selloff on fears that rivals such as AMD and Google could gain market share in AI servers if NVIDIA's roadmap slipped. 
Adding to the positive sentiment, The Information reported that China may allow leading AI companies, including Alibaba, ByteDance, and DeepSeek, to purchase limited quantities of NVIDIA's H200 AI chips, potentially providing a modest boost to demand despite ongoing U.S. export restrictions. 
Goldman Sachs also reiterated a bullish view, arguing that NVIDIA's forward P/E of roughly 21.7x is attractive, trading near the broader S&P 500 average and well below the company's own five-year historical multiple.

A U.S. federal judge on Wednesday approved the $1.5 billion settlement between the Securities and Exchange Commission (SEC) and Elon Musk over his 2022 purchases of Twitter shares, while expressing significant reservations about the agreement. U.S. District Judge Sparkle Sooknanan said she had "serious doubts" about the settlement but concluded that her role was limited to determining whether it met basic standards of fairness and reasonableness, adding that the public—not the court—should ultimately judge whether the SEC held Musk appropriately accountable. 
The settlement resolves the SEC's lawsuit alleging that Musk failed to timely disclose that he had acquired a 5% stake in Twitter in April 2022, allowing him to continue buying shares at lower prices and allegedly save about $150 million. Six months later, Musk completed his $44 billion acquisition of Twitter, which was later rebranded as X.
